data.title: Dividend King PepsiCo's Q1 Shows That Its Turnaround Isn't Just Talk
data.description:
After sitting out of the bull market for quite some time, PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) bulls finally got the quarter they've been waiting for, and I don't think the market is processing what it actually means for PEP. This morning, PEP delivered $19.44 billion of revenue in Q1, which exceeded the consensus estimates by $500 million, and $1.61 of non-GAAP EPS, which was a beat of $0.06. This is no longer a situation where management is telling a story about transformation, as Q1 2026 gave the market a lot to be excited about. What could be the most important aspect is that PEP believes its organic revenue will grow between 2-4% in 2026. Regardless of whether we see a rotation back into big tech, PEP is one of the most recognizable brands globally with products that can’t be vibe-coded away. It would be nearly impossible to recreate PEP’s organizational reach, and the valuation at these levels looks inexpensive, trading at around 18 times 2026 earnings while producing a dividend yield that exceeds 3.5%. I am very bullish on what I read this morning from PEP and think the valuation at a $211.67 billion market cap is very attractive, especially in a market where the worst from the geopolitical conflict overseas could be behind us.

Following up on my previous article about PEP

Back in January, I had written an article on PEP (can be read here) where I discussed why I was still bullish on PEP under $140. I felt that PEP traded at a compelling valuation, trading at less than 20 times earnings with a dividend yield that exceeded 4%. I believed that PEP's operational turnaround was dependent on margin expansion via cost cuts, AI, robotics, and lower commodity pricing, not aggressive top-line growth. Management was prioritizing cost structure optimization and portfolio simplification, which were two things I felt would be extremely beneficial to the bull narrative. After going through Q1 earnings, I believe that PEP is very undervalued and trades at a great value for long-term investors. The market just got confirmation that the turnaround is working, and I think that PEP will generate further alpha for investors with a growing dividend that yields over 3.5%.

The Q1 2026 earnings print was exactly what the market needed and investors wanted

This was an impressive quarter for PEP, as its revenue came in at $19.44 billion, which was up 8.5% YoY and was $500 million ahead of estimates. PEP’s net revenue growth was constructed from a contribution of 2.6% from organic growth, 3.4% from foreign exchange translation, and 2.5% from the net benefit of acquisitions and divestitures. On the bottom line, PEP’s core EPS of $1.61 beat the $1.55 consensus by $0.06 and grew 9% YoY. The reported EPS of $1.70 was up 27% YoY due to favorable mark-to-market gains on commodity derivatives and the reduced restructuring charges. When I look at the operating profit, it tells a similar story, as PEP generated $3.21 billion, which was up 24% YoY, with their operating margin expanding 210 basis points to 16.5%. Their core operating profit was $3.05 billion, which was up 9% YoY, while the core operating margin increased 10 basis points to 15.7%. All the operating margin expansion came from SG&A leverage, as SG&A grew 1.5% as their revenue increased 8.5%. In my opinion, this indicates that the productivity narrative is showing up exactly where I would expect it to. For the past 6 months, management has been telling investors that the 2026 margin expansion story was going to be about operating leverage, not pricing power, and that is what the Q1 numbers are indicating.

The segment data tells a more interesting story than the 8.5% headline revenue number because the performance wasn't uniform across the portfolio. PFNA did $6.33 billion in revenue, which was up 2% YoY. PEP’s convenient foods volume turned positive with 2% growth, which was the first positive volume print in the past several quarters and one of the most important data points in the earnings release. PBNA was very strong, as the beverage division generated $6.39 billion in revenue, which was up 9% YoY due to M&A activity, but on an organic level, the growth was still 2%. The international businesses were where PEP shone in Q1, as the Asia Pacific division generated 7% organic revenue growth with 9% volume growth and 30% core constant currency operating profit growth. EMEA came in at $2.82 billion with 7% organic growth, 9% convenient foods volume, and 17% core constant currency operating profit growth. The IB Franchise delivered 5% of organic growth. When I put all of this together, it indicates that the international business is expanding, the North American segments are stabilizing, and the narrative about PEP being broken should be in the past.

Management reaffirmed PEP’s 2026 guidance for organic revenue growth of 2% to 4%, core constant currency EPS growth of 4% to 6%, a core effective tax rate of approximately 22%, free cash flow (FCF) conversion of at least 80%, and total cash returns to shareholders of approximately $8.9 billion. The Q1 organic growth came in at 2.6%, which was right in the middle of the full-year range, and with management reaffirming a 2-to-4 organic range after a 2.6 Q1 print, they're effectively saying organic growth will remain between the 2-to-4 band for the rest of the year. PEP traditionally has easier comps in Q2 and Q3, and when I include the productivity savings trajectory and the potential oil tailwind, I would be surprised if they don’t land above 3% growth for the remainder of the year. My bull case also compounds the same logic for the core EPS, as management has a range of 4-6% for 2026, and after a 5% print, I think they can come in at the top of their range or even exceed it.

From a valuation perspective, PEP looks enticing, and the market is overlooking this

When I think about value, I want to see that a company is returning capital to its shareholders through buybacks and dividends and that it is trading at a discount based on its earnings power. In 2025, PEP returned $8.75 billion of capital back to shareholders, making them true partners in the business. PEP paid out $7.64 billion in dividends while buying back $1.11 billion worth of shares. When I look at the dividend, this is what I expect from a Dividend King. PEP is paying a dividend of $5.69 per share, and they just announced their 54th consecutive annualized increase, adding another 4% to the dividend. In 2026, PEP is expecting to allocate $8.9 billion towards its return of capital program, which will be comprised of $7.9 billion in dividends and $1 billion in buybacks. PEP is paying investors a dividend yield that exceeds a 3.5% yield while making their shares represent a larger portion of the earnings and revenue with every share repurchased. The dividend growth is unlikely to stop any time soon, and I am very bullish on their ability to continue providing value through their capital allocation plan going forward.

PEP is expected to generate $8.60 of EPS this year, placing it at 18.4 times earnings. Over the next 2 years, PEP is expected to grow its EPS by 14.10% ($1.21) and trades at 16.1 times 2028 earnings. When I look at The Coca-Cola Company (KO) and compare it to PEP, PEP looks undervalued from an earnings perspective. KO is expected to produce $3.22 of EPS this year and trades at 23.3 times earnings. Over the next 20 years, KO has similar growth at 14.50% and trades at 20.4 times 2028 earnings. I think that the combination of the earnings growth, the multiple on earnings, buybacks, and the dividend being paid makes PEP an undervalued gem in a market that is discounting how AI can be utilized in the future to expand margins.

The risks to my investment thesis for PEP

While I am bullish on PEP, there are significant risk factors to consider. The PFNA division could stall again, and margin compression could happen if oil prices don’t decline, causing input prices to remain elevated for an extended period of time. In the U.S., GLP-1 adoption continues to gain momentum, and this can become a long-duration overhang on snack demand as Americans start to change their lifestyles. There is no guarantee that PEP’s momentum in the turnaround will continue in the following quarters, and if the turnaround story fades, PEP may be looked at as an untouchable stock compared to a tech industry that is expanding the top and bottom line by double digits. If PEP doesn’t succeed, their multiples on earnings could compress even further. Investors should do their own due diligence on PEP before making any investment decisions.

Conclusion

I felt that the Q1 print for PEP was very strong, and they set themselves up for a strong 2026 fiscal year. PEP is establishing reaccelerating organic growth, a North America that's showing early signs of a volume turn, a productivity engine that's expected to hit its peak contribution this year, and paying a dividend that just increased for the 54th consecutive year. Management reaffirmed guidance, and it feels like they are being conservative considering the beat on the Q1 numbers. Investors got a clean top and bottom line beat today on Q1 earnings, 2.6% of organic growth, and a $1 billion YoY improvement in operating cash flow. I believe that the turnaround was on full display in Q1, and this should continue throughout the year. I think shares of PEP are inexpensive, trading under 20 times this year's earnings, especially when investors are getting a compounding dividend and billions in buybacks.

data.title: Automatic Data Processing Might Be Cheap For A Reason
data.description:
From time to time, I screen for stocks that might be interesting and fit my investment criteria of high-quality businesses with a wide economic moat. And when looking for companies with stable margins and high return on invested capital, we will find, at some point, Automatic Data Processing, Inc. (ADP). However, ADP is also showing up in other screenings due to the steep declines in the last few months. Presently, ADP is among the top 5% of worst performers in the S&P 500 over the last six months and has declined 40% from its previous all-time high.

In the following article, we will analyze ADP and determine whether the company is actually a business with a wide economic moat and whether the stock is a good investment at this point. We will see that ADP might have a wide economic moat around its business. However, there is a risk that the moat might erode, and high-growth rates are still necessary for the stock to be clearly undervalued. And with the rising risk of GenAI being a challenge to ADP, I don’t know whether we should assume such high growth rates.

Company Description

As always, when writing about a business for the first time, we start with a business description. If you are already familiar with the business, you can skip this section.

Automatic Data Processing is an American provider of cloud-based human resource management and payroll processing services. The software application company was founded in 1949 and had its IPO in 1980. Today, the company has about 67,000 employees and generates about $20 billion in annual revenue. The company has about 1.1 million customers and is processing the payroll for about 42 million people in 140 countries around the world, and the employee tax statements for about 78 million people.

The business is reporting in two different segments:

  • Employer Services: This segment offers human resources outsourcing solutions, including software platforms for small business payroll, HR, and compliance services. This segment also offers software for the tracking of time and attendance and tools for talent management, and was responsible for about two-thirds of total revenue. Currently, the segment is reporting a margin of around 35%.
  • Professional Employer Organization: While Employer Services mostly offers software and tools for its customers, the second segment offers outsourcing services to customers, and parts of HR functions are outsourced. This segment is generating about one-third of the company’s total revenue, and this segment is reporting only a margin between 13% and 15% in the last few quarters.

Second Quarter Results

On January 28, 2026, ADP reported second-quarter results, and the company did beat analysts’ estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share. Revenue increased from $5,048.4 million in Q2/25 to $5,359.3 million in Q2/26, resulting in 6.2% year-over-year growth. Earnings before income taxes increased 9.7% year-over-year from $1,261.3 million in the previous quarter to $1,383.2 million in this quarter. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $2.35 in Q2/25 to $2.62 in Q2/26, resulting in 11.5% year-over-year bottom-line growth.

When looking at the two different segments, Employer Services grew its revenue 6% year-over-year to $3,607 million in Q2/26, with the margin also improving 50 bps to 35.4%. This margin improvement was mostly driven by operating leverage and client funds interest revenue growth.

The PEO Service segment also increased its revenue 6% year-over-year to $1,755 million, but the margin declined 70 bps from 15.1% in the same quarter last year to 14.4% in the current quarter. Here, it was mostly higher selling expenses that drove margins down.

These are certainly solid results, and there does not seem to be any reason to worry about ADP. Nevertheless, we mentioned above that ADP declined about 40% from its previous all-time high, while the S&P 500 is once again very close to its all-time high. Therefore, investors are obviously worried about ADP and expect the business to struggle in the coming quarters.

Threat 1: Economy Slowing Down?

One reason that is often mentioned is the economy slowing down, which might be a threat to ADP as the business is rather cyclical. When looking at the stock performance above, we see the stock price usually reacting during recessions, indicating that the business might be cyclical.

When looking at the numbers ADP reported in the last few decades, we also see revenue declining in recessions, and earnings per share, as well as free cash flow, are also reacting. However, the picture for EPS and FCF is rather choppy, and it would certainly be a misrepresentation of data to claim that earnings per share or free cash flow decline, especially during recessions.

When looking at the labor market data, we see that the unemployment rate is slowly crawling higher. However, with an unemployment rate of 4.3%, we certainly should not be worried, and with 210,000 initial claims for unemployment insurance (4-week moving average), the labor market seems to be in good shape.

However, the nonfarm payrolls are telling a different story. In the last months, 178,000 people were added, which is a good sign, but since the beginning of 2025, the number has been constantly fluctuating around zero, with several months being negative.

We certainly see hints of hiring slowing down and the labor market struggling a little bit. And these worries might be accelerated by the recent developments. The war with Iran (even if the ceasefire might last) had an impact on oil prices, and as a result, supply chain shocks will probably have an impact on the economy as well. All in all, this is generating a high risk that the economy might continue to struggle in the coming quarters.

Threat 2: Is AI Threatening the Business?

While a slowing-down economy might certainly be considered a risk, it is most likely not the main driver for the steep decline in the last few months. The bigger reason is most likely the AI concerns that have already sent numerous other software stocks down.

In the last few weeks, I looked at various businesses that declined steeply due to concerns about AI attacking these businesses, and these companies were mostly software companies. And when looking at the S&P 500 Heatmap once again (I used the map many times in the last few weeks), we see the overall map is greener now, but a few sectors are still clearly red, which includes software infrastructure companies as well as software application companies. And ADP is one of the software companies that is clearly red and has declined 32% in the past six months.

To determine whether ADP is at risk here and might lose market share due to the threat from GenAI, we look at many metrics once again. We start by looking at revenue growth in the last few years and see the quarterly revenue growth rate slowly declining in the last few months (it is also below the long-term average right now).

Additionally, we can look at margins in the last few years, and we clearly see the gross margin being very stable right now (after improving again from the 2020 temporary lows). Operating margin is fluctuating a bit from quarter to quarter, but I also don’t see signs of the situation getting worse.

And although the business can still keep up, and key metrics do not show signs of trouble, we should not ignore risks for ADP that clearly exist. In the following sections, we are focusing especially on the Employer Services segment, which is not only the most important segment for the business but also especially at risk of disruption.

ADP is offering many HR tasks that are certainly at risk of getting replaced by GenAI applications. Services like payroll and payments, or the tracking of working days and working time, are not so difficult, and it would be neither difficult nor extremely expensive to create applications with similar functionality using GenAI. In theory, I would argue that a giant part of ADP’s software offerings is at risk and can be replaced.

However, only looking at whether it is possible to replace software is not enough, as almost every product or service can be replaced by a competitor. The wide economic moat of companies usually stems from the fact that it is impossible to duplicate the product. It is rather the fact that customers can’t be convinced to switch due to a steep learning curve or the fact that a brand name is associated with luxury and prestige.

Wide Economic Moat

In the following section, we are therefore asking whether ADP has a wide economic moat and whether this moat might be able to protect the business against the GenAI threat. While a moat should not only be determined by looking at numbers and metrics, this is a strong hint for a wide economic moat around a business.

First, we can look at the stock performance of ADP versus the S&P 500, and the argument here is that a stock that can outperform the S&P 500 over the long run (several decades) probably has some kind of advantage that other companies can’t match. ADP clearly outperformed the S&P 500, even when including the last 40% decline.

Another metric we can look at is the company’s margins, which not only improved over the last ten years but are also showing very little fluctuation—a good sign for pricing power and a wide economic moat around the business. Additionally, we can look at return on invested capital—one of the most important metrics to determine whether a business might have a wide economic moat. During the past ten years, ADP reported a RoIC of 35.39% on average, and in the last five years, it was even 41.01% on average. We usually can assume that a company with an RoIC above 10% might have an economic moat.

From a qualitative perspective, we can make the argument that ADP has switching costs preventing customers from choosing an alternative that might be cheaper. The software and application might be embedded in the company’s daily workflow, and workers might be familiar with the software—all this is increasing the switching costs. On the other hand, I would also make the argument that the learning curve for these applications (time tracking or payroll) is probably not as steep, making it much easier to switch to another product.

Although ADP did really report impressive metrics in the past (especially RoIC is extremely high), I would raise the question of how wide the economic moat actually is, and certainly see a risk for the moat eroding over the next few years.

Balance Sheet

While an economic moat might help a business to defend itself against competitors and challenges, the balance sheet is also extremely important. In challenging times, a company should not have too much debt and reasonable interest payments. This enables a company to react to challenges if cash is available and is not used for interest payments and debt repayments.

On December 31, 2025, the company had $3,976.4 million in long-term debt on its balance sheet but no short-term debt. When comparing the total shareholders’ equity of $6,393 million, we get a D/E ratio of 0.62, which is certainly acceptable. And we have to consider that ADP has a huge amount of treasury stock on its balance sheet, which is reducing shareholders’ equity.

Aside from comparing the total debt to shareholders’ equity, we compare it to the generated free cash flow. In the last four quarters, free cash flow was $4,185 million, and ADP could repay its debt in less than one year. Additionally, the company has $2,424.7 million in cash and cash equivalents, as well as $45.7 million in short-term marketable securities, and therefore enough to repay more than 60% of the debt outstanding. Even when considering $3,924.2 million in goodwill, we should not be worried about the balance sheet and the company’s liquidity and solvency at this point.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we calculate an intrinsic value for the stock to determine whether ADP might be a good investment at this point. At first, let’s look at the two simple valuation multiples: the price/earnings ratio and the price/free-cash-flow ratio.

At the time of writing, ADP is trading for 19.0 times earnings as well as 18.7 times free cash flow. These valuation multiples are clearly below the long-term average of 24.89 for the P/E ratio, 23.76 for the P/FCF ratio, and the stock is also trading for the lowest valuation multiples in the past ten years. This indicates that ADP is rather a bargain at this point.

As always, we determine an intrinsic value by also using a discounted cash flow calculation, and we take a 10% discount rate (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least by any investment). Additionally, we use the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (404.7 million in Q2/26). Now we have to determine a free cash flow that is reasonable for the business, and we could use the FCF of the last four quarters (which was $4,185 million). Finally, we must determine a reasonable growth rate for the years to come. We can look at the company’s own mid-term targets, which are rather optimistic, and assume at least 10% growth annually for the next ten years, followed by 4% growth in perpetuity. Calculating with these metrics, we get an intrinsic value of $256.96, and the stock is undervalued right now.

When looking at free cash flow and the free cash flow margin, we see current numbers being above the long-term (15.8%) and the 10-year (16.7%) average, and we could therefore make the argument that the free cash flow might be a bit too high. When using the average free cash flow margin of the last ten years, we would get a free cash flow of $3.55 billion, and this would lead to an intrinsic value of $217.97—all other assumptions being the same.

Conclusion

We can make the argument that ADP is slightly undervalued at this point and might be a good investment. Additionally, I would also make the argument that we have several strong hints for a wide economic moat around ADP’s business, and switching costs are in place.

However, ADP still must grow at least in the high single digits (or maybe even double digits) in the next ten years to be fairly valued or slightly undervalued. Looking at the company’s own targets and past growth rates, it might seem possible—but we should also consider the scenario of ADP being pressured by competitors using GenAI to offer similar services more cheaply, and this might actually lead to lower growth rates. In the end, I see ADP rather as a “Hold” right now, and I consider lower stock prices being possible in the coming quarters.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Reed Hastings Logs Out Of Netflix (Are You Sure?)
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Reed Hastings will leave as chairman of Netflix in June. (0:15) Federal regulators to look at MLB online distribution deals. (1:44) OpenAI commits over $20B to Cerebras chip-powered AI servers. (2:33)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Netflix (NFLX) is tumbling premarket on softer-than-expected guidance and news that Chairman, co-founder and former CEO Reed Hastings will leave the company’s board in June.

Hastings, who helped transform Netflix from mailing DVDs to customers into the gold standard of streaming, said he is shifting his focus to philanthropy and other pursuits. He co-founded the company in 1997 and served as CEO for more than two decades before handing the role to co-CEOs Ted Sarandos and Greg Peters.

In Q1, Netflix posted EPS of $1.23, though the figure is not comparable to the Street’s $0.76 estimate because it includes a $2.8B Warner merger breakup fee tied to Paramount Skydance (PSKY).

Revenue rose 16% Y/Y to $12.25B, topping expectations of $12.17B, reflecting stronger subscription growth, pricing gains and higher ad revenue.

However, investors had hoped for a lift to full-year guidance now that the Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) battle is behind it. Instead, Netflix maintained its revenue outlook of $50.7B to $51.7B, with the midpoint below the $51.38B consensus estimate. For Q2, the company guided to an operating margin of 32.6%, down 150 basis points from a year earlier.

Seeking Alpha analyst Max Greve said Netflix has been “feasting on the upside of ads and sports,” but warned that engagement friction, league dependency and scripted crowding were becoming more apparent.

“This report makes all those negative trends manifest,” he added.

Regulators are examining the distribution of streaming rights for Major League Baseball as part of a broader federal probe into how professional sports leagues deliver games to online platforms.

FCC Chairman Brendan Carr told Bloomberg he has been hearing from fans frustrated by the fragmented viewing landscape.

“One thing that did surprise me was the level of interest of baseball fans in particular,” he said.

The scrutiny follows a Wall Street Journal report that the Department of Justice is investigating whether the NFL’s practice of splitting broadcast rights across networks and platforms is anticompetitive and harmful to consumers.

“You could make the argument that there are other sports leagues out there that are potentially pushing the limits of the Sports Broadcasting Act even further than what the NFL has,” Carr added. “The NFL is something that everyone is aware of and focuses on. … But we are focused more broadly on other leagues as well.”

OpenAI has agreed to pay chip designer Cerebras (CBRS) more than $20B over the next three years to use servers powered by the company’s chips, according to The Information.

As part of the deal, OpenAI will receive warrants for a minority stake in Cerebras, with its ownership potentially increasing as spending rises. OpenAI has also agreed to provide roughly $1B to help fund development of datacenters running its AI products.

Total spending under the agreement could reach $30B over three years, which would give OpenAI warrants equivalent to as much as 10% of Cerebras.

Now Here’s What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Western states are exploring desalination deals as the Colorado River crisis deepens.

Citi strategist Scott Chronert is warning that a “reverse perfect storm” could shape earnings season.

And Albemarle (ALB) is climbing toward a three-year high as a rebound in lithium prices fuels momentum.

In premarket trading, stock index futures (SPX) (US100:IND) (INDU) are slightly higher as traders look to close out another strong week for bulls.

The S&P 500 (SP500) is on track to gain more than 3% for a third-straight week.

While attention has centered on hopes that the conflict with Iran could wind down sooner rather than later, former J.P. Morgan strategist Marko Kolanovic says the rally appears to be driven more by systematic CTA flows than geopolitics. He notes that oil (CL1.COM), closely tied to Iran developments, is roughly where it stood at the end of March, even as stocks have added about 10%.

And it’s all quiet on the economic front, with no major releases scheduled.

data.title: U.S. Coal Rides Wave Of Soaring Global Gas Prices
data.description:
The war in the Middle East and related disruptions to Qatari LNG exports have triggered fuel switching from gas to coal, especially in regions facing LNG supply risk. This has been particularly pronounced in Europe, where coal prices and demand spiked as utilities sought alternatives to expensive and less available gas. In the Pacific Basin, South Korea lifted coal-burning restrictions in response to LNG supply concerns, and Japan maintained high thermal coal imports, both supporting regional coal demand. India's government furthermore ordered increased coal-fired generation and the restart of major plants to meet expected peak power demand, with coal imports set to rise if LNG supplies remain tight and as the summer approaches. While these developments have pushed seaborne coal demand higher, resulting price increases also reflect growing dry bulk freight rates due to higher marine diesel costs, constraining margins on delivered coal.

US coal demand has strengthened modestly, as increased competitiveness against natural gas boosts power-sector consumption. This is attributed to higher power needs from sectors such as centers, delays in new gas generation deployments and elevated natural gas prices, making coal more attractive for utilities and supporting stable or rising coal production through at least 2027. Federal policy actions — including production credits for metallurgical coal, reduced royalty rates, delayed coal plant retirements, and funding for modernization — have underpinned a more stable demand and regulatory landscape for coal. Firmer natural gas prices are also expected to support stable coal generation via gas-to-coal switching through 2027. After 2027, the US coal market is forecast to face pressure from the expansion of zero-carbon electricity in regions with supportive economics and state policies.

In the longer term, the S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast projects coal plant retirements and reduced utilization. By 2035, 36.5 gigawatts of coal plants are forecast to retire. Coal-plant generation share through 2035 is forecast to decline to 8.4% from 18.3% of total generation in 2026.

The take

  • Coal prices rose markedly to end March, with international demand and higher shipping costs impacting US export prices.
  • Near-term tailwinds for coal include anticipated electricity demand growth and supportive federal policies, while countervailing pressure includes elevated domestic coal inventories and competitive natural gas in some regions of the US.
  • In the longer term, S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast projects 36.5 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, amid market pressure to retain existing coal and natural gas generation. Market pressure is attributed to several factors, including elevated natural gas prices, high demand growth and delays in deploying new gas generation.

The expanding Middle East war drove US coal prices higher across the board during March, with gas-to-coal switching and higher seaborne freight costs both contributing. Export benchmarks saw the most price movement, as CAPP region export benchmarks gained $4.00/short ton to $87.00/short ton (4.8%); NYMEX CAPP added $3.50/st (4.3%) to $85.00/st; and NAPP Pittsburgh Seam 13,000 British thermal units/pound jumped $5.25/st to $70.75/st (8.0%). Gains to domestic coal were more measured, with Illinois Basin 11,500 mid-sulfur adding $1.25/st to $55.25/st (2.3%) and the NYMEX Powder River Basin benchmark holding at $15.00/st.

Although global natural gas prices surged, warmer weather in March led to an easing in US natural gas prices. On the Gulf Coast, stronger LNG demand was offset by growth in associated natural gas volumes as Gulf and Texas onshore oil producers ramped up to meet a globally constrained crude oil market. Henry Hub spot prices opened March at $2.99/million Btu and moved to a mid-month high of $3.27/MMBtu before falling to close the month at $2.88/MMBtu. Spot prices averaged $3.05/MMBtu for the month. Storage withdrawals continued through March, more slowly than normal, with working gas at 1,865 billion cubic feet as of March 27. This was 54 Bcf above the five-year average and 96 Bcf above the same week of 2025.

As winter came to an end and natural gas demand shifted to the Gulf Coast, regional gas market discounts mostly held steady. Chicago Gate averaged $2.67/MMBtu, a 38-cent/MMBtu discount to Henry Hub. TCO Pool moved to a discount of 64 cents/MMBtu, for a monthly average of $2.41/MMBtu, while TETCO M3 moved to a discount of 57 cents/MMBtu for a monthly average of $2.48/MMBtu. The Western US began the year with mild weather and higher hydroelectric generation, to which a surplus of associated natural gas out of West Texas has been added. During March, these factors drove SoCal Border to a nationwide low of $1.24/MMBtu during the month, $1.81/MMBtu below Henry Hub.

The US Energy Information Administration (EIA) estimated December 2025 coal stockpiles at 109 million st, decreasing by 3 million st from November. While we forecast growth in coal demand during the year, this modest inventory surplus will tend to restrain production growth.

Current forward pricing for PRB coal has been flat and stable, reflecting sufficient inventories at power plants and mining capacity to increase production as needed against firmer natural gas prices. After 2027, lower natural gas prices and declining coal demand are forecast to restrain price growth.

Bituminous coal price levels are primarily influenced by export markets, with today's price levels making domestic coal generation generally less competitive against Northeast natural gas. The current disruption in international energy markets has elevated export coal prices, likely through 2027. As natural gas prices normalize after that, we project some easing in coal prices.

Pricing benchmarks exceeding $70/st suggest sustainable returns for eastern bituminous coal, with Atlantic Basin export coal above that threshold and coal competing in Pacific Basin export markets generally much closer. Bituminous coal demand for electric generation is expected to remain stable through 2027 on higher electricity demand and supportive natural gas prices. Declines in steam coal demand are expected to resume after 2027, and overall Eastern US coal demand is forecast to decline 47 million st in 2025–30.

Outlook for US coal production, demand

For the four weeks ending March 28, coal shipments averaged 10.4 million st, just 2.1% below the same week in 2025. Heading into spring, sufficient inventories and low seasonal demand appear likely to constrain production and shipments to levels at or below 10 million st per week.

The chart below compares the current production forecast with recent history. We forecast increased coal demand against higher natural gas prices through 2027, with annual production at 550 million st. Beginning with 2028, relative normalization of coal and gas pricing and growth in green generation is forecast to pressure coal generation lower. The overall coal market, including domestic demand and exports, is forecast to decline by 139 million st between 2026 and 2031.

Production outlook — Powder River Basin

Production reports of the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) covering the fourth quarter of 2025 indicate total 2025 production of 230.0 million st. Production for 2026 is forecast at 243 million st, 5.7% higher than 2025. Production is forecast to remain elevated through 2027 against higher natural gas prices. By 2030, S&P Global Energy projects that coal retirements in the Midwest and expansion of wind generation in PRB's core markets will gradually shrink the coal demand to 204 million st, declining further to 177 million st through 2035.

Production outlook — Illinois Basin

MSHA's 2025 fourth-quarter production reports indicate total 2025 production of 66.2 million st. We forecast production will grow to 75 million st through 2027, after which the expansion of wind generation and announced coal retirements are forecast to erode the ILB coal demand. Coal production in the ILB is forecast to fall to 65 million st by 2030, declining further to 50 million st by 2035.

Production outlook — Appalachian basins

MSHA's production reports for the year's fourth quarter indicate year-end production at 159.3 million st. Appalachian coal demand tends to be more sensitive to global seaborne markets than to domestic natural gas prices, compared to the PRB or the ILB, which are forecast for improved demand against natural gas generation; therefore, gains in Appalachian coal will be more limited. We forecast modest declines from 2025 levels through 2027. As remaining domestic demand erodes after 2027, with only modest offsets from export growth, Appalachian production is forecast to fall to 112 million st by 2030.

Further information

Market Indicative coal forecasts by S&P Global Energy represent forward curves for spot-traded instruments, analogous to a strip of contracts. The shorter tenors — current year and prompt year, plus additional years, if available — are driven by the observed/assessed marker prices. The longer tenors — typically forecast years three to 20 for physically assessed markers — are driven by fundamental estimates of cash costs of production, accepted returns to capital, regional productive capacity, and forecast supply and demand. For the long-tenured portion of the curve, S&P Global Energy forecasts prices for specific coal markers and defines the remaining markers via historical spreads.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
